comment_text.iti48:4.1: Kāsāvakaṇṭhā is usually taken as “ochre-necks” with kaṇṭha in the sense “throat”, but the commentary is not so clear: “It seems that, having tied a single ochre scrap (khaṇḍa) around their hand or neck (gīvā) they wander about.” Thus while the commentary mentions the neck, it directly glosses kaṇṭha with khaṇḍa, a term for a scrap or portion; eg. a cammakhaṇḍa is an item made from scraps of leather (Bu Pc 15:2.1.8). Khaṇḍa is doubly appropriate, since it is a common word for “broken” precepts.

root_text.iti48:2.3: Yo ca abrahmacārī brahmacāripaṭiñño, yo ca paripuṇṇaṁ parisuddhaṁ brahmacariyaṁ carantaṁ amūlakena abrahmacariyena anuddhaṁseti.
root_text.iti48:2.4: Ime kho, bhikkhave, dve āpāyikā nerayikā idamappahāyā”ti.
root_text.iti48:3.2: Yo vāpi katvā na karomi cāha;
root_text.iti48:6.1: Ayampi attho vutto bhagavatā, iti me sutanti.

translation_text.iti48:1.1: This was said by the Buddha, the Perfected One: that is what I heard.
translation_text.iti48:2.1: “Mendicants, these two are bound for a place of loss, bound for hell, if they don’t give up this fault.
translation_text.iti48:2.3: Someone who is unchaste, but claims to be chaste; and someone who makes a groundless accusation of unchastity against a person whose chastity is pure.
translation_text.iti48:2.4: These are the two who are bound for a place of loss, bound for hell, if they don’t give up this fault.”
translation_text.iti48:3.2: as does one who denies what they did.
translation_text.iti48:3.3: Both are equal in the hereafter,
translation_text.iti48:4.1: Many who wear a scrap of ocher cloth
translation_text.iti48:4.3: Being wicked, they are reborn in hell
translation_text.iti48:5.1: It’d be better for the immoral and unrestrained
translation_text.iti48:5.4: than to eat the nation’s alms.”
translation_text.iti48:6.1: This too is a matter that was spoken by the Blessed One: that is what I heard.
